title: Income Tax Act 2025
focus: Key provisions of the new Income Tax Act 2025, including MAT, AMT, and the inclusion of virtual digital assets.
rationale: This section covers a major legislative update. The distinctions between MAT and AMT, along with the expanded definition of undisclosed income to include virtual digital assets, are highly testable for UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: The President has given her assent to the Income Tax Act 2025 which replaces the Income-Tax Act, 1961. The provisions of Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) and Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) have been separated into two sub-sections. MAT aims to bring into the tax net 'zero tax companies'... The provisions of MAT are applicable to a corporate taxpayer only. AMT has similar provisions as MAT which is applicable to non-corporate tax payers. Undisclosed Income: The definition of undisclosed income... is expanded to include virtual digital assets.
evidenceBullets: The Income Tax Act 2025 replaces the Income-Tax Act, 1961.
Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) applies exclusively to corporate taxpayers (zero tax companies).
Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) applies to non-corporate taxpayers.
The definition of undisclosed income for search cases now includes virtual digital assets.
Income tax authorities can access virtual digital spaces (e.g., email servers, social media) during search and seizure proceedings.
A dispute resolution panel is available for eligible assessees, including those in transfer pricing cases, non-residents, and foreign companies.

title: Safe Harbour Rules and Advance Pricing Agreements (APAs)
focus: CBDT's expansion of safe harbour rules and the framework of APAs for transfer pricing.
rationale: Transfer pricing, APAs, and Safe Harbour rules form a cohesive cluster of corporate taxation concepts frequently tested in economics and finance sections.
sectionExcerpt: Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) notifies amendments in Income-Tax Rules, 1962 to expand the scope of safe harbour rules. Increasing threshold for availing safe harbour from Rs. 200 Crore to Rs. 300 Crore. Including Lithium-Ion Batteries for use in electric or hybrid electric vehicles in definition of core auto components... Advance Pricing Agreements (APAs)... aims to provide certainty to taxpayers in the area of transfer pricing by specifying pricing methods and determining the arm's length price of international transactions.
evidenceBullets: The threshold for availing safe harbour was increased from Rs. 200 Crore to Rs. 300 Crore.
Lithium-Ion Batteries for electric/hybrid vehicles are now included in the definition of core auto components for safe harbour.
Safe harbour means the tax authority accepts the transfer price declared by the taxpayer to be at arm's length.
An Advance Pricing Agreement (APA) determines the arm's length price of international transactions for a maximum term of 5 years with no minimum period.
Transfer Pricing refers to the price of goods and services exchanged between companies under common ownership or control.
The Arm's Length Principle states that prices in related-party transactions must match prices in comparable unrelated-party transactions.

title: Securities Transaction Tax (STT)
focus: Definition, applicability, and purpose of the Securities Transaction Tax.
rationale: STT is a specific direct tax instrument with clear applicability rules (equity, derivatives, mutual funds) suitable for standalone objective questions.
sectionExcerpt: Securities Transaction Tax (STT) - It is a direct tax levied on transactions of securities conducted through a recognized stock exchange in India. Taxable securities include equity, derivatives, and unit of equity-oriented mutual fund. It is also applied on the transaction of options and futures. Introduced under the Finance Act, 2004. Aim: to curb tax evasion in the securities market.
evidenceBullets: STT is a direct tax levied on securities transactions conducted through recognized stock exchanges in India.
Taxable securities under STT include equity, derivatives, equity-oriented mutual funds, options, and futures.
STT was introduced under the Finance Act, 2004 to curb tax evasion in the securities market.

title: Goods and Services Tax (GST) Reform
focus: Evolution from GST 1.0 to GST 2.0, including constitutional backing, tax structure, and destination-based nature.
rationale: GST fundamentals and the transition to a two-slab structure (GST 2.0) are critical indirect tax topics for UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: The 56th meeting of the GST Council has approved Next-Gen GST reform (GST 2.0) that includes rate rationalization with a simplified two-slab structure. Evaluation of GST 1.0 - About GST: It is a single indirect tax on the supply of goods and services across India. It was Introduced by the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2017. It subsumed 17 different taxes and 13 cesses into one unified tax... Destination-Based Tax: Revenue goes to the state/UT where the goods or services are finally consumed.
evidenceBullets: GST 2.0 aims for rate rationalization with a simplified two-slab structure.
GST is a single indirect tax introduced by the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2017.
GST subsumed 17 different taxes and 13 cesses, eliminating the cascading effect of taxes.
GST is a destination-based tax, meaning revenue goes to the state or UT where goods/services are finally consumed.
IGST is levied on inter-state transactions, collected by the Centre, and shared with the states.
